title: The Final Best Picture Power Rankings and a ‘Sinners’ Second Look With Autumn Durald Arkapaw!
format: Sean and Amanda work through a wide-open BAFTA slate, catch the dumped Glen Powell caper 'How to Make a Killing,' revisit 'Sinners' a year out, and file their last Best Picture power rankings before the PGAs and SAGs. Then Sean talks cameras, aspect ratios, and center-punching with 'Sinners' cinematographer Autumn Durald Arkapaw.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: Took Best Film, Director, Cinematography, Adapted Screenplay, Editing, and a Supporting Actor win for Sean Penn. Sean still isn't fully sold on it over Sinners for Best Picture, and notes BAFTA has matched the Oscar only twice in 14 years.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Three BAFTAs: Supporting Actress (Wunmi Mosaku), Original Screenplay, and Score, more than either host expected and a sign of up-the-ballot strength.

films.title: I Swear
films.note: Robert Aramayo won Actor for this true story of a man with Tourette's; the film cleaned up at BAFTA but isn't out stateside until April, another wrinkle in a Best Actor race they'd thought was settled.

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: Jessie Buckley's Actress campaign stays dominant, and the film won Best British Film, which briefly raised, then dropped, the idea of a three-horse Best Picture race.

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: Won Best Film Not in the English Language, a real challenge to The Secret Agent in the International Feature race.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Went 0-for-11 on the night, enough to raise the specter of it becoming this year's Irishman.

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Won Production Design, Makeup and Hair, and Costume Design; they expect a repeat at the Oscars.

films.title: F1
films.note: Took Sound. Amanda floats a Ford v Ferrari-style editing path for it too, so long as cars are going fast.

films.title: Avatar: Fire and Ash
films.note: Won Special Visual Effects. Big Jim, as expected.

films.title: How to Make a Killing
films.note: John Patton Ford's follow-up to Emily the Criminal, a soft riff on Kind Hearts and Coronets with Glen Powell as a disowned heir murdering his way back to the family fortune. Dumped into under 2,000 theaters despite a wide-release tag. Both hosts liked it. Sean calls it a 6-or-7 that goes down easy, praises Zach Woods, and dings a tonal clash between farce and class tragedy; Amanda flags Margaret Qualley overdoing it. Set in Powell-free Huntington, Long Island, Sean's hometown.

segments.heading: ‘Sinners,’ a year later
segments.note: the most nominated film in Oscar history, revisited

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Amanda's first full rewatch since theaters: she finds the vampire set pieces the least memorable part and the Smoke-vs-Klan climax and the 'I lied to you' musical sequence the high points. Both read the vampires as a metaphor for the ownership of Black art, note it's the non-Black women who keep inviting the vampires in, and single out Delroy Lindo's garlic-clove scene, Jack O'Connell's Rocky Road to Dublin number, and Michael B. Jordan's twin performance.

films.title: From Dusk Till Dawn
films.note: The Rodriguez/Tarantino vampire-bar movie people keep reducing Sinners to; Sean argues it belongs to a longer lineage rather than being a straight lift.

films.title: Near Dark
films.note: Kathryn Bigelow's vampire film, cited as an ancestor of From Dusk Till Dawn and, by extension, Sinners.

films.title: Assault on Precinct 13
films.note: John Carpenter's siege movie, another structural forebear Sean traces the lineage through.

films.title: The Thing
films.note: Carpenter's is-anyone-who-they-say monster movie. Sean ties the Delroy Lindo garlic test and the Cornbread-at-the-door scene straight to it, and the interview confirms it's Coogler's stated reference.

segments.heading: Best Picture Power Rankings
segments.note: the final edition, before the PGAs and SAGs
segments.subject: the 2026 Best Picture race

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: No. 1, unmoved. Sean's favorite movie of the year and the closest thing to a lock, though a big Sinners night at the PGA and SAG could flip him.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Holds at 2 with 16 nominations and real momentum; two early acting wins on Oscar night could rewrite the whole race.

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: At 3. Almost certainly wins nothing beyond Best Actress, but the affection is real.

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: At 4, favored for International Feature and with a live shot in a supporting category; Coogler thanked Joachim Trier as a mentor in his BAFTA speech.

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Up to 5 on the strength of two probable craft wins.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Slides to 6 after an 0-for-11 BAFTA night, but could still take Best Actor.

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: At 7. Sean won't yet call International Feature against it; the film hits Hulu March 1st, when they'll do a fuller conversation.

films.title: F1
films.note: Up to 8. Sean's logic is it has a stronger shot in two categories (sound, editing) than Train Dreams has in one.

films.title: Train Dreams
films.note: Down to 9. A strong, Malick-adjacent cinematography case, but likely leaves the night empty-handed.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: Falls to last at 10 for winning nothing; both enjoyed the Lanthimos-Emma Stone reunion more than Poor Things anyway.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: The Oscar-nominated cinematographer on shooting dual formats (65mm 5-perf for the 2.76:1 Ultra Panavision 70 extraction and 15-perf for the 1.43:1 IMAX), on center-punching every character so the extraction never crops a head, on top-lighting to a moonlight source and lighting dark skin without over-lighting it, and on the burning-river finale as the hardest scene. Her second film with Ryan Coogler; she's the first woman of color nominated for Best Cinematography.

films.title: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
films.note: Her first Coogler collaboration, a year-long shoot with underwater work that built the shorthand she and the recurring department heads now run on.

films.title: The Last Emperor
films.note: The film that hit her at 9 or 10, her first taste of scope and feeling small inside a movie's world.

films.title: Raging Bull
films.note: Seen in an LMU genre course; the black-and-white contrast and real faces set her toward figuring out what a DP actually does.

films.title: Broadway Danny Rose
films.note: The other film from that same course that hooked her; she wrote a paper on both.

films.title: Manhattan
films.note: The anamorphic look she couldn't place as a student, the origin of her lifelong preference for the format and Panavision glass.

films.title: Palo Alto
films.note: Gia Coppola's debut, which she shot after filling in on a test-shoot day; it pigeonholed her in indie teen movies for a while before the scope work came.

films.title: The Last Showgirl
films.note: The 18-day Gia Coppola shoot she was making when Coogler sent her the Sinners script; she read it the night before shooting and emailed her thoughts.

films.title: The Thing
films.note: Coogler's stated reference for Sinners: anamorphic, moody, textured. She draws the tension from it rather than copying its look.

films.title: The Hateful Eight
films.note: Her touchstone for the Ultra Panavision 70 format she'd used since film school.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: The IMAX bank sequence that convinced her nothing beats that camera once it's in your hands.

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: She compares the IMAX camera's demands on actors to Lol Crawley operating VistaVision for Brady Corbet, a return to how movies used to be made.

films.title: Lawrence of Arabia
films.note: Her last-great-thing pick, rewatched while prepping a David Lean documentary interview; the lone figure rising up center-punched at the well is the frame she keeps in her head.

referenced.group: ‘How to Make a Killing’ in context
